vowels.t: Short open back vowel. Very common in Kanien'kéha words. Do not add an English r-colour after it. IPA: [a].
The colon (:) always marks vowel length. A long vowel can completely change a word's meaning — never shorten it. IPA: [aː].
Short mid front vowel. Keep it pure — no gliding into 'ay' as in English. IPA: [e].
Held roughly twice as long as the short version. IPA: [eː].
High front vowel, like Spanish 'i'. Short and crisp. In Ontario dialects (Six Nations, Tyendinaga), some positions use 'y' where Kanehsatà:ke uses 'i' — both are valid. IPA: [i].
Rounded back vowel. Short unless marked with :. IPA: [o].
Length here distinguishes verb forms and possession. Always honour the colon. IPA: [oː].

Air flows through both mouth and nose simultaneously. Nasal vowels are a key feature of Iroquoian languages. The TTS approximation uses "an" but the n should not be heard as a separate consonant — it just signals nasalization. IPA: [ã].
Nasalized rounded back vowel. ę [ã] and ǫ [õ] are distinct — both common in Kanehsatà:ke Kanien'kéha. IPA: [õ].

consonants.t: Unaspirated velar stop — no puff of air after it, unlike English 'key'. Think of the 'k' in 'sky' or 'ski'. IPA: [k].
Unaspirated alveolar stop. The 't' in 'stop', not 'top'. IPA: [t].

A full consonant in Kanien'kéha, not just punctuation. The throat closes briefly — exactly like the pause in English 'uh-oh'. It appears word-initially, mid-word, and word-finally, and changes meaning. IPA: [ʔ].
Glottal fricative. Breathy onset before vowels; can also appear between vowels. IPA: [h].
Standard alveolar nasal consonant, similar to English. IPA: [n].
Alveolar flap [ɾ] in Kanehsatà:ke and Kahnawà:ke; lateral [l] in Akwesasne. Both are valid community-level dialect features, not errors. IPA: [ɾ] or [l].
Labial-velar approximant. Smooth glide into the following vowel, similar to English. IPA: [w].

consonants.s: palatal glide; y in Ontario, i in Quebec
consonants.t: Palatal approximant [j] in Six Nations/Tyendinaga; high front vocoid [i] in Kanehsatà:ke/Kahnawà:ke in the same positions. Both are correct dialect forms. IPA: [j] or [i].
Alveolar affricate — a stop immediately followed by a fricative. Particularly consistent in Kahnawà:ke. IPA: [ts].
Palato-alveolar affricate, like English 'ch' in 'church'. IPA: [tʃ].

tone.t: The colon (:) in the orthography corresponds to the IPA length mark [ː]. Always write it; always pronounce it. A long vowel is held roughly twice as long as its short counterpart, and the distinction changes meaning. IPA: [Vː].

tone.s: full consonant — written as ' in orthography
tone.t: The apostrophe (') in Kanien'kéha orthography represents the glottal stop [ʔ]. It appears mid-word and word-finally. í:'i (I) and í:se' (you) are different words partly because of its position. IPA: [ʔ].

The acute accent (á, é, í, ó) marks primary stress. Stressed syllables are louder and higher in pitch. In IPA, stress is marked with [ˈ] before the stressed syllable. Kanien'kéha pitch-stress patterns can change grammatical meaning.

Both ę [ã] and ǫ [õ] are nasalized vowels distinctive to Kanien'kéha. Air passes through both mouth and nose. They are separate phonemes — ę is low and central, ǫ is rounded and back. Both are common in Kanehsatà:ke speech.

The prefix R- (Ro-, Ra-, Ron-, Rok-, Ran-) marks male person nouns and pronouns in Kanehsatà:ke/Kahnawà:ke. In Akwesasne this becomes L- (Lo-, La-, Lon-, Lok-, Lan-). Compare I-/Iak-/A-/Ako- for female.

The prefixes I-, Iak-, A-, and Ako- mark female person nouns and pronouns. The specific prefix depends on the noun class and social register — Ako- is formal/respectful (mothers, elders), aónha is informal. This system is fully systematic throughout the language.
